additionalTime: 10 minutes potato steam-dry time, 10 minutes chile soak, 10 minutes sauce rest while the filling cooks, plus 3 to 5 minutes foil rest
author: Robert Carr
description: A focused sausage, egg, potato, and cheese breakfast burrito with crisp-edged Yukon Gold potatoes, deeply browned sage-forward breakfast sausage, tender low-and-slow scrambled eggs, a three-cheese melt, toasted 10-inch flour tortillas, and a glossy simmered guajillo-arbol drip sauce served warm on the side for adding to each bite.
equipment: Potato saucepan: medium saucepan for parboiling potatoes
Main skillet: large 12-inch heavy skillet or saute pan for chiles, sauce vegetables, sausage, potatoes, tortillas, and burrito toasting
Sauce saucepan: small saucepan for simmering the hot sauce
Egg skillet: nonstick skillet for eggs, optional but helpful
Rimmed baking sheet or wire rack, optional but helpful for steam-drying potatoes
ingredients: 10 ounces Yukon Gold potatoes, cut into 1/2-inch cubes
2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the potato water
1/8 teaspoon baking soda for the potato water
1 1/2 teaspoons cornstarch
1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika
1/2 teaspoon garlic powder
1/4 teaspoon onion powder
1/4 teaspoon cumin for the potatoes
1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the potatoes, or 1/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1/2 teaspoon black pepper for the potatoes
6 ounces bulk pork breakfast sausage, preferably sage-forward
1 tablespoon avocado oil for the potatoes, only as needed
4 large eggs
1 1/2 tablespoons half-and-half
1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the eggs, or 1/8 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1/4 teaspoon black pepper for the eggs
1 tablespoon unsalted butter
2 ounces sharp cheddar cheese, freshly shredded
2 ounces Monterey Jack cheese, freshly shredded
3 slices American cheese
3 fresh, pliable 10-inch flour tortillas
2 dried guajillo chiles, stemmed and seeded
2 dried chile de arbol peppers, stems removed
2 tomatillos, husked and rinsed
1 Roma tomato
1 jalapeno
1/8 small white onion
2 garlic cloves, unpeeled
1 1/2 tablespoons apple cider vinegar for the hot sauce
1 tablespoon fresh lime juice for the hot sauce
1/4 cup chile soaking water or fresh hot water for blending, plus more as needed
1 tablespoon avocado oil for frying the hot sauce
1/2 teaspoon tomato paste for the hot sauce
1/2 teaspoon honey
1/2 teaspoon cumin for the hot sauce
1/4 teaspoon Mexican oregano for the hot sauce
1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt for the hot sauce, or 1/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt
1/2 teaspoon black pepper for the hot sauce
instructions: Place the 10 ounces diced Yukon Gold potatoes in the potato saucepan and cover them with cold water by 1 inch.
Add 2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt and 1/8 teaspoon baking soda to the potato saucepan.

Bring the potatoes to a boil over high heat, then reduce the heat and simmer for 5 to 6 minutes, until the potato edges just begin to look rough and the centers are barely tender but not falling apart.
Drain the potatoes very well, return them to the empty warm saucepan, and shake the pan gently for 15 to 20 seconds to rough up the starchy potato edges without breaking the cubes apart.

Spread the potatoes in a single layer on paper towels, a wire rack, or a rimmed baking sheet, pat the tops dry if they look wet, and let them steam-dry for 10 minutes, until the surfaces look matte instead of steamy. Do not rinse them; the rough starch is what crisps.
While the potatoes dry, make the simmered guajillo-arbol breakfast drip sauce.

Toast 2 stemmed and seeded dried guajillo chiles and 2 stemmed dried chile de arbol peppers in the dry main skillet over medium heat for 15 to 30 seconds per side, turning often, just until fragrant and slightly softened, not scorched. If any chile smells burned or turns black, discard it and toast another.
Transfer the toasted chiles to a small bowl, cover them with hot water, and soak for 10 minutes.
While the chiles soak, place 2 tomatillos, 1 Roma tomato, 1 jalapeno, 1/8 small white onion, and 2 unpeeled garlic cloves in the dry main skillet over medium-high heat.

Cook the hot sauce vegetables for 8 to 12 minutes, turning occasionally, until the tomatillos soften, the garlic is browned and soft inside, and the tomatoes, jalapenos, and onion are charred in places.
Peel the roasted garlic cloves and stem the roasted jalapeno. For a milder sauce, split the jalapeno and scrape out some or all of the seeds and ribs before blending.
Wipe the main skillet clean if any chile flakes or dark vegetable residue remain, then set it aside for the sausage and potatoes.
Drain the soaked chiles, reserving the soaking water.
Taste the reserved chile soaking water. If it tastes bitter or harsh, use fresh hot water for blending and thinning instead.
Transfer the soaked chiles to a blender.

Add the roasted tomatillos, tomato, stemmed jalapeno, onion, peeled garlic, 1 1/2 tablespoons apple cider vinegar, 1 tablespoon fresh lime juice, 1/4 cup chile soaking water or fresh hot water, 1/2 teaspoon honey, 1/2 teaspoon cumin, 1/4 teaspoon Mexican oregano, 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/2 teaspoon black pepper to the blender.

Blend the hot sauce on high for 35 to 60 seconds until very smooth and slightly lighter in color, adding 1 to 2 tablespoons more chile soaking water or fresh hot water only if needed. The sauce should be loose enough to drip from a spoon but not watery.
For a smoother drip sauce, pass the blended hot sauce through a fine-mesh strainer before simmering; for a more rustic sauce, leave it unstrained.
Heat 1 tablespoon avocado oil in the sauce saucepan over medium heat.
Add 1/2 teaspoon tomato paste to the hot oil and stir for 30 to 45 seconds, until it darkens slightly and smells savory.
Carefully pour the blended or strained hot sauce into the hot tomato paste oil; it may splatter at first.

Simmer the hot sauce over medium-low heat for 5 to 7 minutes, stirring often, until the raw dried-chile flavor softens, the vinegar and lime round off, the color deepens slightly, the oil looks integrated, and the sauce lightly coats a spoon but still drips easily; you should have about 1 to 1 1/4 cups sauce.
If the hot sauce gets too thick, whisk in chile soaking water or fresh hot water 1 tablespoon at a time until it drips easily again.
Taste the hot sauce and adjust only if needed with a small pinch of kosher salt, a few drops of lime juice, or a few drops of honey.
Cover the hot sauce off heat for at least 10 minutes while you cook the burrito filling so the toasted chile, vinegar, and lime settle into one sauce.

Cook 6 ounces bulk pork breakfast sausage in the main skillet over medium heat for 6 to 8 minutes, breaking it into pea-size to nickel-size crumbles and letting the crumbles sit against the pan long enough to brown, until deeply browned and fully cooked. If the sausage releases liquid before it browns, keep cooking until the liquid evaporates and the sausage starts frying in its own fat.
Transfer the sausage to a paper towel-lined plate with a slotted spoon and spread it out so steam does not soften the browned edges.
Reserve 1 tablespoon of sausage fat and any browned fond that is not scorched in the main skillet and discard the rest, or add 1 tablespoon avocado oil if the sausage did not release enough fat.

Toss the steam-dried potatoes with 1 1/2 teaspoons cornstarch, 1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika, 1/2 teaspoon garlic powder, 1/4 teaspoon onion powder, 1/4 teaspoon cumin, 1/2 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/2 teaspoon black pepper.
Heat the reserved sausage fat in the main skillet over medium-high heat until hot.
Add the seasoned potatoes to the main skillet in a single layer, using a second skillet or working in batches if needed so they are not crowded.

Cook the potatoes, or each batch, undisturbed for 3 minutes so the first crust sets, then turn every 2 to 3 minutes, scraping up browned sausage bits only after the potatoes release cleanly, for 10 to 14 minutes total, until deeply golden and crisp on multiple sides.
Transfer the crispy potatoes to a plate in a shallow layer and keep them uncovered so steam does not soften the crust.
Taste one potato cube while it is still hot and add a tiny pinch of kosher salt only if it tastes flat.
Whisk 4 large eggs with 1 1/2 tablespoons half-and-half, 1/4 teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt, and 1/4 teaspoon black pepper until no streaks of egg white remain.
Melt 1 tablespoon unsalted butter in the egg skillet over medium-low heat. For minimum cookware, wipe out the main skillet, let it cool to medium-low, and use that instead.
Add the eggs and cook gently, folding with a rubber spatula, until the eggs form soft curds and are just set but still slightly looser than you want in the finished burrito.
Immediately transfer the eggs to a plate and spread them into a shallow layer for 1 to 2 minutes so leftover heat slows down and excess steam escapes.
If any watery egg liquid collects on the plate, tilt it away or blot it lightly so the burritos stay tidy.
Mix the shredded cheddar and Monterey Jack together, then divide the crispy potatoes, browned sausage, soft scrambled eggs, and shredded cheese blend into 3 equal portions before rolling.

If the sausage or potatoes have cooled completely, return them to the warm main skillet for 30 to 60 seconds just to take the chill off before assembly; keep the potatoes in a shallow layer so they do not steam.
Wipe the main skillet clean if needed, then warm each 10-inch flour tortilla in the dry skillet for 20 to 30 seconds per side, or until soft, flexible, and lightly toasted in spots.
If a tortilla feels stiff or cracks when flexed, sprinkle it with a few drops of water, warm it a few seconds longer, and keep it covered before filling.
Stack the warmed tortillas in foil or a clean kitchen towel so they stay soft while you assemble the burritos.
Assemble 1 burrito at a time by placing a warm tortilla on a clean work surface.
Tear 1 slice American cheese in half and overlap the pieces just below the center of the tortilla so the melt runs the length of the filling strip.
Keep the filling in a compact strip just below the center of the tortilla, leaving clear side margins so the 10-inch tortilla can close cleanly.
Sprinkle about half of 1 portion of the sharp cheddar cheese and Monterey Jack cheese over the American cheese.
Add 1 portion of the browned breakfast sausage over the cheese.
Add 1 portion of the crispy potatoes over the sausage.
Add 1 portion of the soft scrambled eggs over the potatoes.
Sprinkle the remaining half portion of sharp cheddar cheese and Monterey Jack cheese over the eggs so the burrito melts and binds from both sides.
Fold the left and right sides of the tortilla over the filling, then roll tightly from the bottom into a compact burrito.
Place the burrito seam-side down in the dry main skillet over medium heat.

Toast the burrito for 1 to 2 minutes per side, starting with the seam side and pressing lightly, until lightly crisp, sealed, and browned in spots. If the tortilla browns before the cheese has time to melt, lower the heat slightly for the remaining sides.
Wrap the toasted burrito in foil and let it rest for 3 to 5 minutes so the cheese finishes melting and the filling settles.
Repeat with the remaining 2 tortillas and fillings.
Rewarm the simmered guajillo-arbol breakfast drip sauce over low heat if needed, thinning with 1 tablespoon chile soaking water or fresh hot water at a time until it drips easily again.
Serve each burrito with the warm simmered guajillo-arbol breakfast drip sauce on the side.
Drip a small spoonful of hot sauce onto each bite as you eat instead of pouring it inside the burrito.
notes: The minimum cookware setup is the main skillet, potato saucepan, and sauce saucepan. The egg skillet makes the egg step easier, but it is not required. If using the main skillet for eggs, wipe it clean after the potatoes, let it cool to medium-low, use the full 1 tablespoon butter, and fold the eggs gently with a rubber spatula.
This recipe intentionally keeps the burrito filling limited to sausage, egg, potato, and cheese. The hot sauce is served outside the burrito and does not count as an internal filling.

The reduced filling ratio is intentional. Three burritos made with 10-inch tortillas, 10 ounces potatoes, 6 ounces sausage, 4 eggs, and the listed cheese roll cleaner and eat with better balance than overstuffed larger burritos.
Bulk pork breakfast sausage is better than sliced sausage links because it browns into small crumbles that distribute evenly through the burrito.
A sage-forward breakfast sausage gives the most classic breakfast flavor. Avoid maple breakfast sausage unless you specifically want a sweeter burrito.
Reserve only enough sausage fat to crisp the potatoes. Too much loose grease makes the burrito heavy.
Yukon Gold potatoes are the best choice because they get creamy inside and crisp outside without turning mealy.
The small pinch of baking soda in the potato water softens the potato exteriors just enough to create rough, starchy edges; keep the simmer short so the cubes do not crumble.
The quick pan shake after draining roughs up those starchy potato edges so the cornstarch clings and crisps harder.
The cornstarch coating is a small but important crispness boost. It helps the potatoes brown and gives them a rough, crisp exterior.
The small amount of cumin in the potato seasoning ties the filling to the guajillo-arbol sauce without making the burrito taste like taco seasoning.
The potatoes should be crisp before they go into the burrito because they soften slightly after the foil rest.
Do not crowd the potatoes in the skillet. A crowded pan steams the potato cubes and prevents the rough cornstarch-coated edges from turning crisp.
Do not overcook the eggs until dry. The target is soft, tender curds with no wet liquid pooling in the pan.
Spreading the eggs on a plate before assembly prevents leftover heat from making them dry and lets extra steam escape before the burrito is wrapped.
Freshly shredded cheddar and Monterey Jack melt better than pre-shredded cheese. The American cheese is included for melt, binding, and burrito-shop texture.
Splitting the shredded cheese below and above the hot filling helps the burrito hold together without adding sauce inside the tortilla.
Use fresh, pliable 10-inch flour tortillas and divide the filling evenly among 3 burritos. Smaller or dry tortillas will be difficult to roll with this much filling.
Do not overfill the burritos. A tightly rolled slightly smaller burrito eats better than a huge torn one.
Toast the assembled burrito seam-side down first. It seals the fold, melts the cheese, and gives the tortilla a lightly crisp exterior.
The foil rest matters. It helps the cheese fully melt, lets the burrito hold together, and gives the tortilla a better breakfast-shop texture.
Keep the hot sauce on the side if you want the tortilla to stay firmer. Drip it onto each bite while eating.
The guajillo chiles give the sauce body, color, and deeper red chile flavor without making it aggressively hot. The chile de arbol peppers provide the sharper heat.
Do not use scorched dried chiles. A few dark spots on roasted vegetables are good, but blackened dried chiles can make the sauce bitter.
If the chile soaking water tastes bitter, do not use it. Fresh hot water gives the sauce a cleaner finished flavor.
Briefly cooking the tomato paste in the oil gives the hot sauce a rounder roasted flavor and keeps it from tasting thin next to the eggs and cheese.
Simmering the blended hot sauce in oil makes it taste more finished, less raw, and more like a burrito-shop sauce.
The 10-minute hot sauce rest rounds off the toasted chile, vinegar, and lime so the sauce tastes integrated on eggs and potatoes.
The finished hot sauce should taste a little brighter and saltier than you would want by itself because the tortilla, potatoes, eggs, and cheese mellow it out.
For a milder hot sauce, remove the jalapeno seeds and use only 1 dried chile de arbol pepper, or skip the dried chile de arbol peppers entirely for the mildest version.
For a hotter hot sauce, use 4 to 5 dried chile de arbol peppers or add 1 serrano.
The hot sauce should be loose enough to drip but not watery. Add chile soaking water or fresh hot water 1 tablespoon at a time only after blending, simmering, or reheating.
Warm filling is part of the cheese strategy. The American cheese and split shredded cheese melt best when the sausage, potatoes, and eggs are still warm before the skillet toast and foil rest.

These burritos are best eaten immediately after the foil rest, but leftovers can be cooled with the sauce kept separate, wrapped tightly in foil, and reheated in a 350 degrees F oven until hot. Unwrap and toast briefly in a dry skillet if the tortilla needs to crisp back up.
title: Sausage, Egg, Potato, and Cheese Breakfast Burritos with Simmered Guajillo-Arbol Drip Sauce
